text.title: ab-testing Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for ab-testing as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Stopping the test early when results look good. "We reached significance on day 4, so we shipped."
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Early stopping is p-hacking. Sequential testing inflates false positive rates unless using sequential analysis corrections (Pocock or O'Brien-Fleming boundaries). The pre-calculated sample size accounts for day-of-week effects. Stopping early misses them.

failure-mode.description: Running too many variants at once. "Let us test button color (3 options), size (2 options), and copy (2 options) simultaneously." That is 12 combinations.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Multivariate testing requires exponentially larger sample sizes. 12 combinations need 12x the traffic to detect the same effect size as a single A/B test. Run sequential tests instead: narrow down button color first, then test size on the winning color.

failure-mode.description: Ignoring the novelty effect. "Variant got 25% lift in week 1; ship it!"
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: New UI elements get initial clicks simply because they are new. The lift often decays after week 1 as novelty wears off. The test duration must be long enough to observe the decay if it exists.

failure-mode.description: Confusing statistical significance with practical significance. "The result is significant at p=0.04, so it is important."
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Statistical significance says the effect is real, not that it matters. A 0.1% lift at 99% confidence may not be worth implementing if the engineering cost is high. Always check both the confidence interval and the practical magnitude.

failure-mode.description: Novelty effect: new UI elements get clicks simply because they are new; watch for lift that decays after week 1.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Participants or users see something new and engage more, but the lift is temporary. Running tests for at least one full business cycle reveals whether novelty effects decay or persist.

failure-mode.description: Simpson's paradox: aggregate results can hide segment reversals. Desktop users prefer variant A, mobile users prefer variant B.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: The overall winner may not be the best variant for all segments. Always analyze key segments before concluding.

failure-mode.description: Interaction effects: running two simultaneous tests on overlapping user populations creates confounding.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: If variant A is running on feature X and variant B is running on feature Y, and the same users see both, you cannot isolate which variant drove the result. Randomize users across tests to prevent overlaps or stagger tests.

failure-mode.description: Statistical significance is not practical significance: a 0.1% lift at 99% confidence may not be worth implementing if engineering cost is high.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Always check both the confidence interval (is the effect real?) and the practical magnitude (is it worth doing?). Statistical significance answers the first question only.

failure-mode.description: Tests run too short surface false positives; stopping at "significance" without reaching pre-calculated sample size is p-hacking.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: The pre-calculated sample size accounts for day-of-week effects, business cycles, and traffic variation. Stopping early misses these effects and inflates false positive rates.
